coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric fluorescence visual test paper for an anthrax biomarker based on functionalized manganese-doped carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Rong, Mingcong
Liang, Yucong
Zhao, Danli
Chen, Baiji
Pan, Chen
Deng, Xiangzhou
Chen, Yibo
He, Jin
coredata.dc:description: Functionalized manganese-doped carbon dots (FMn-CDs) were prepared by integration of pyrolysis-derived Mn-CDs with ethylene diamine, ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid and Eu(III) ions. The FMn-CDs emitted intense blue fluorescence with high photostability, excellent water-solubility and favorable biocompatibility. 2, 6-dipicolinic acid (DPA) is an important biomarker of Bacillus anthracis spore. It serves as an antenna suction light group and can sensitize the Eu(III) combined on the FMn-CDs to produce bright red fluorescence. Benefitting from the absorbance energy transfer emission effect from DPA to the sensitized Eu(III), a ratiometric biosensor towards DPA detection was developed. The approach exhibited a linearity range from 0.1 to 750 nM and a detection limit of 0.1 nM. Moreover, visual FMn-CDs test paper was produced for the DPA detection using a smartphone under a portable UV lamp, with a lowest detection limit of 1 μM. This work revealed the potential of a convenient smart phone diagnostics for DPA detection using visual FMn-CDs test paper.
coredata.dcterms:subject: FMn-CDs
Ratiometric fluorescence biosensor
Dipicolinic acid
Visual FMn-CDs test paper

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the visual FMn-CDs test paper for DPA assay
Ratiometric fluorescence detection of DPA using the FMn-CDs
Visual detection for DPA using the FMn-CDs test papers

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangzhou City
Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangdong Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was financially supported by the Guangdong Natural Science Foundation (No. 2017A030310652 and 2017A030313255 ), Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangzhou City ( 201804010117 ), Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangdong Province (No. 2011A060901013 ) and Guangzhou University’s training program for excellent new-recruited doctors ( YB201717 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme illustration of the visual detection of DPA using the FMn-CDs test paper.

(A) TEM and HRTEM images of the FMn-CDs. Inset: the corresponding size distribution of the FMn-CDs. (B) FT-IR spectrum, (C) XPS spectrum, (D) C 1s spectrum, (E) N 1s spectrum, (F) Mn 2p spectrum and (G) Eu 3d spectrum of the FMn-CDs.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of x%Mn-CDs (the excitation wavelength was 365 nm). (B) QY of x%Mn-CDs. (C) UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the FMn-CDs. Inset: photographs of the solution of the FMn-CDs without (left) and with (right) 60 μM DPA under a 254 nm UV lamp. (D) The emission spectra of the FMn-CDs under different excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 to 400 nm.

(A) Fluorescence responses of the FMn-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of DPA. Inset: The linear relationship between F616nm/F461nm and the concentration of DPA ranging from 0 to 750 nM. (B) Fluorescence responses of the FMn-CDs (F616nm/F461nm) in 60 μM various interferents without and with 750 nM DPA. (C) Time-resolved decay of fluorescence emission at 461 nm of the FMn-CDs with different concentrations of DPA. Inset: lifetime values of fluorescence emission at 461 nm of the FMn-CDs with different concentrations of DPA. (D) Time-resolved decay of fluorescence emission at 616 nm of the FMn-CDs with different concentrations of DPA. Inset: lifetime values of fluorescence emission at 616 nm of the FMn-CDs with different concentrations of DPA.

Fluorescence color images taken by the smartphone of the FMn-CDs test paper-based visual assay of different concentrations (from left to right: 0, 1, 10, 50, 100, 150 and 200 μM) of DPA under a 254 nm UV lamp. And the corresponding relationship between R/B and concentration of DPA scan by an APP of the smartphone using the FMn-CDs test paper.
Determination of DPA spiked in natural water samples (n = 3).
Determination of DPA spiked in real samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A ratiometric fluorescence visual test paper for an anthrax biomarker based on functionalized manganese-doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangzhou University, Guangzhou, Guangdong, 510006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Functionalized manganese-doped carbon dots (FMn-CDs) were prepared by integration of pyrolysis-derived Mn-CDs with ethylene diamine, ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid and Eu(III) ions. The FMn-CDs emitted intense blue fluorescence with high photostability, excellent water-solubility and favorable biocompatibility. 2, 6-dipicolinic acid (DPA) is an important biomarker of Bacillus anthracis spore. It serves as an antenna suction light group and can sensitize the Eu(III) combined on the FMn-CDs to produce bright red fluorescence. Benefitting from the absorbance energy transfer emission effect from DPA to the sensitized Eu(III), a ratiometric biosensor towards DPA detection was developed. The approach exhibited a linearity range from 0.1 to 750 nM and a detection limit of 0.1 nM. Moreover, visual FMn-CDs test paper was produced for the DPA detection using a smartphone under a portable UV lamp, with a lowest detection limit of 1 μM. This work revealed the potential of a convenient smart phone diagnostics for DPA detection using visual FMn-CDs test paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacillus anthracis spores, has become a particular biological threat concern over the past decades. Inhalation of more than 104 of its spores leads to death within 36 h [1,2]. 2, 6-dipicolinic acid (DPA) is an anthrax biomarker of these bacterial spores, occupying 5–15% of the dry mass of the spore. Thus, it is essential to develop rapid and sensitive detection methods for DPA [3,4]. Accordingly, a variety of methods have been developed for the DPA detection. Meso-droplets supported on super hydrophobic wires combined with surface-enhanced Raman measurements, an automated sample preparation system coupled with gas chromatography-mass spectrometry and high-performance liquid chromatographic method with a reversed-phase column have been used to detect DPA [5–7]. High affinity binding of DPA to lanthanide ions (especially Eu(III) and Tb(III)) enables the DPA detection with merits such as bright lanthanide ions fluorescence, long fluorescence lifetime, large stokes shifts and high sensitivity. Under UV excitation and upon DPA coordination, the fluorescence intensity of DPA-sensitized lanthanide ions yields an increase by more than several orders of magnitude through an absorbance-energy transfer-emission (AETE) effect [8–10]. Ratiometric fluorescence method, is generally composed by a constant built-in reference signal and a sensitive response signal. In recent years, ratiometric fluorescence method has become a popular accurate and sensitive monitoring method owing to its background-free and self-calibrating merits. Fluorescence methods based on metal organic frameworks, carbon nanomaterials and polymer quantum dots have been applied in the DPA detection due to their sensitivity and selectivity [3,4,11–17]. However, most of them are carried out by a single test mode or rely on large instruments, which limited their widespread applications. Therefore, a portable and convenient method for DPA detection is urgently in need.

Carbon dots (CDs) have received extensive attention due to their excellent fluorescence properties, photostability, low toxicity and favorable biocompatibility. Owing to these fascinating advantages, CDs are widely used in the areas of bioimaging, drug delivery, photocatalytic devices and chemical sensing [18]. Since the discovery of CDs, many synthetic strategies have been devised to regulate their electronic characteristic and optical properties [18–23]. One of the most effective ways is heteroatom doping [24]. N-doping was reported to enhance the emission of CDs by upward shift of the Fermi level and electrons in the conduction band [25]. Mg, N-codoped CDs was reported to enhance the fluorescence of CDs [26]. Mn (II)-doped CDs with core-state-dominated yellow fluorescence was demonstrated by using metal-coordination complexes [27]. With the thriving of CDs in bioimaging and chemical sensing, more facile synthetic methods are needed to obtain heteroatom doped CDs with better optical properties and new functions.

Herein, fluorescent manganese-doped CDs (Mn-CDs) were prepared by one-pot pyrolysis synthesis using citric acid and manganese chloride. With a proper doping content, Mn-doping enhanced the fluorescence and quantum yield (QY) of the Mn-CDs. Functionalized Mn-CDs (FMn-CDs) were obtained by the combination of EA, EDTA and Eu(III) with the pyrolysis synthesized Mn-CDs. The FMn-CDs emitted intense blue fluorescence with excellent water-solubility, high photostability and excellent biocompatibility. Using the red fluorescence (616 nm) of DPA sensitized Eu(III) as the fluorescence response signal, and the blue fluorescence (461 nm) of FMn-CDs as the fluorescence reference signal, a ratiometric fluorescence detection method towards DPA was developed. Furthermore, the proposed approach has potential application in real samples and visual detection of DPA with FMn-CDs test paper by a smartphone.

Europium nitrate hexahydrate, manganese chloride and citric acid were from Aladin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 2, 6-dipicolinic acid (DPA), nicotinic acid (NA), picolinic acid (PA), isonicotinic acid (ISA), phthalate (PH) were from Sigma-Aldrich, Inc. (U.S.). Quinine sulfate, ethylenediamine (EA), ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA) and fiber filters were from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were in the analytical grade. Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was from Gibco, Inc. (U.S.). All solutions were prepared by ultrapure water (with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ·cm) obtained from a Millipore purification system (Millipore, U.S.).

The Mn-CDs were prepared based on previous report with minor modification [28]. Different amount of manganese chloride (0, 0.01, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5 and 1 mmol) and 1 mmol citric acid were added in round-bottom flasks and heated at 225 °C for 9 min. The mixture solid firstly turned into transparent liquid, then became a yellow liquid, undergoing pyrolysis and carbonization, finally became an orange mixture. After the mixture cooled down, some ultrapure water and NaOH were added. The solution was then transferred into a dialysis bag (MWCO: 1k) and dialyzed for one day to obtain the pale yellow Mn-CDs solution. Since the fluorescence intensity and QY of 25% MnCl2-derived Mn-CDs were the optimum, they were used in the following experiments.

The FMn-CDs were prepared by a typical process [2]. In brief, the Mn-CDs solution were reacted with 0.1 mL EA for 2 h, then 1 mL EDTA (0.1 M) was added and reacted for another 2 h. The mixture solution was transferred into a dialysis bag (MWCO: 1k) and dialyzed for one day. Finally, 1 mL Eu(NO3)3 (0.1 M) was added into the dialyzed solution and stirred for 2 h. After dialysis for another day, the FMn-CDs were obtained. The final concentration of the FMn-CDs was 200 μg/mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the visual FMn-CDs test paper for DPA assay
ce:section.ce:para: Fiber filter paper was used to prepare the FMn-CDs test paper for visual DPA detection. A hole puncher was used to fabricate circular strips with a diameter about 6 mm. 5 μL of 500 μg/mL FMn-CDs was dropped onto the circular strip, and they were dried in air for 30 min. Then, 5 μL DPA solution with different concentrations (0, 1, 10, 50, 100, 150 and 200 μM) was dropped onto the circular strip. The fluorescence color of the strips can be observed with bare eye under a 254 nm UV lamp in a dark case. The fluorescence photos were taken with a smartphone. The R and B data (RGB color model) of the photos can be quickly obtained using the smartphone (Huawei glory 9) with a color-scanning app (Photoshop, Adobe). The concentrations of DPA were quickly obtained using the calculator in the smartphone according to the standard linear equation. Three parallel experiments were conducted to obtain the reliable results.
The detail DPA detection and cell imaging process were illustrated in the supporting information.

Citric acid is a common CDs precursor because of its abundant carboxyl groups, and Mn(II) is a transition metal ion with 3 d atomic orbital which can accept electrons. Mn-CDs were prepared by pyrolysis and condensation of citric acid and manganese chloride. The FMn-CDs were obtained after three steps of post-functionalization. The morphology, chemical composition and structure of FMn-CDs were characterized by TEM, XRD, FT-IR and XPS measurements. The TEM image of the FMn-CDs showed a narrow diameter ranging from 2.8 to 4.3 nm, with an average size of 3.5 nm (Fig. 1 A). The HRTEM image revealed an interplanar space of 0.221 nm of an individual FMn-CDs, corresponding to the diffraction plane of graphitic carbon [29]. The XRD pattern of the FMn-CDs displayed a broad diffraction peak centered at around 23° (Fig. S1), which ascribed to carbon-based nanomaterial [30]. As displayed in Fig. 1B, the absorption band around 662 cm−1 was ascribed to Mn-O bond [31], the absorption band around 779 cm−1 corresponded to Eu-O bond [32]. The absorption bands around 1060 and 1220 cm−1 were attributed to CO and COH bonds. The absorption bands around 1550–1621 and 1714 cm−1 were attributed to CC and CO bonds [33]. The absorption bands around 3000–3600 cm−1 corresponded to NH and OH bonds [34,35]. The results revealed that there are abundant carboxyl groups on the FMn-CDs. As shown in Figs. 1C to 1G, the FMn-CDs were comprised of C, N, O, Mn and Eu elements with an atomic ratio of C/N/O/Mn/Eu = 0.539/0.175/0.264/0.007/0.015. The C 1s spectrum showed CC/CC (284.8 eV), CO, CN (286.3 eV) and CO (288.1 eV) three bonds [36]. The N 1s spectrum showed NC (399.3 eV), CNH (401.1 eV) two bonds and the charging effects (406.3 eV) [34,37]. The Mn 2p spectrum showed two peaks at 642.2 and 654.0 eV, corresponding to Mn 2p3/2 and Mn 2p1/2, suggesting that Mn is in the presence of tetravalent state and combined with the external carboxyl of the FMn-CDs [38]. The Eu 3d spectrum showed two peaks at 1134.6 and 1164.0 eV, attributed to Eu 3d5/2 and Eu 3d3/2 [39]. The results revealed that Eu was in the presence of trivalent state and combined with the external oxygen-containing groups of the FMn-CDs. The hydrated ionic radius of the FMn-CDs was about 6.8 nm, suggesting their good monodispersity (Fig. S2). In addition, the FMn-CDs displayed good water-solubility (Fig. S3), owing to their external hydrophilic groups. The FMn-CDs were stored at room temperature for 6 months without any precipitation and their hydrated ionic radius remained unchanged (Fig. S2), revealing their excellent stability.

Fluorescence emission spectra and QYs of the Mn-CDs with different Mn doping content (x%) were studied in detail. As shown in Fig. 2 A, their fluorescence emission spectra were similar with a peak emission around 475 nm. Their fluorescence intensity firstly increased with the increase of the Mn content and then gradually decreased, reaching the maximum value at 25% Mn doping content. The QYs of 0%-100%Mn-CDs were 0.083, 0.115, 0.134, 0.135, 0.108 and 0.108 (Fig. 2B and Table S1), the variation of QY values was in according with the corresponding fluorescence intensity. The results implied that the doping of Mn(IV) (from the results of Mn 2p spectrum) can partly enhance the fluorescence intensity and QY of the CDs. Since the fluorescence maximum emission wavelength maintained constant with different Mn(IV) doping content, it is speculated that the pyrolysis and decarboxylation process of citric acid initially occurred to form the internal luminescence groups. Then, a certain amount of Mn(IV) interacted with the external oxygen-containing groups of CDs and occupied the external defects, giving rise to the enhancement of fluorescence intensity and the QY [27,38]. Enough Mn-source will expand the contact area of Mn with nucleated CDs, but excessive Mn-source will disturb the pyrolysis of citric acid and the nucleation process of CDs. To achieve better detection results, FMn-CDs with 25% Mn-doping content were used in the following experiments. The optical properties of the FMn-CDs were investigated by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra measurements. The FMn-CDs showed an absorption band at 335 nm, which derived from the luminescence sp2 clusters in the FMn-CDs (Fig. 2C) [28]. The maximum fluorescence excitation and emission wavelengths were 375 and 487 nm. Under the irradiation of a 254 nm UV lamp, the FMn-CDs emitted intense blue and red fluorescence without and with 60 μM DPA (the inset photo in Fig. 2C). This also indicated the feasibility of visual fluorescence ratiometric sensing of DPA. When the excitation wavelength changed from 300 to 440 nm, the corresponding fluorescence emission peaks of the FMn-CDs shifted from 470 to 535 nm (Fig. 2D). The FMn-CDs exhibited favorable photostability since their fluorescence intensity remained 92.1% of the original intensity after continuous irradiation for 1 h (Fig. S4). To obtain optimal conditions for analytical assays, the effect of pH to the fluorescence property of the FMn-CDs was investigated. The fluorescence intensity of the FMn-CDs gradually increased with increasing the value of pH (Fig. S5), and maintained constant at neutral environment. Finally, pH 7.0 was chosen for the following experiments. The resistance to ionic strength of the FMn-CDs was examined. The fluorescence intensity of the FMn-CDs remained constant even in 1 M NaCl solution (Fig. S6). These results indicated the potential application of the FMn-CDs in real sample detection and their fluorescence bioimaging under physiological conditions.

As a potential fluorescence imaging material, the FMn-CDs were applied in cell imaging. Cytotoxicity is a fundamental factor for cell imaging application. The cytotoxicity of the FMn-CDs was evaluated by the viability of HeLa cells after incubation with the FMn-CDs using a MTT assay. The cell viability remained higher than 96% even when the concentration of the FMn-CDs was up to 200 μg/mL (Fig. S7), confirming that the excellent biocompatibility of the FMn-CDs. Additionally, 100 μg/mL FMn-CDs were used for the cell imaging as well. As shown in Fig. S8, the HeLa cells incubated with FMn-CDs maintained the normal cell activity and morphology. Different from the dark control experiments, bright blue fluorescence can be observed inside the cells, mainly in the cytoplasm. These results implied that the FMn-CDs were internalized by the HeLa cells and could be used as an efficient bioimaging fluorescence material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence detection of DPA using the FMn-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Currently, ratiometric fluorescence detection method has received extensive attention among fluorescence methods because of its higher sensitivity, background-free and self-calibrating performance [40]. In the DPA detection, the FMn-CDs emitted only an intense blue fluorescence (peak at 461 nm) without the addition of DPA. With the addition of DPA (a tridentate ligand to Eu(III)), DPA coordinated with the FMn-CDs and an intense red fluorescence appeared (Scheme 1 ). The blue fluorescence derives from the luminescence sp2 clusters of the FMn-CDs and serves as the fluorescence reference signal. DPA has an absorption band at 270 nm (Fig. S9), so it acts as the antennas group to absorb the 270 nm excitation light and then transfer the energy to Eu(III) on the FMn-CDs. The sensitized Eu(III)-DPA complex emits intense red fluorescence (593 nm and 616 nm, derive from 5D0 to 7FJ, J = 0, 1, 2 of Eu(III)), which serves as the fluorescence response signal towards DPA [1,2,41]. As shown in Fig. S10, the red fluorescence from sensitized Eu(III)-DPA complex appeared immediately (within 1 min) after the addition of DPA, and the blue fluorescence of the FMn-CDs remained nearly constant during the whole process. With the increased concentration of DPA, the red fluorescence from sensitized Eu(III)-DPA complex at 593 and 616 nm gradually increased, and the blue fluorescence of the FMn-CDs maintained almost constant (Fig. 3 A). A fluorescence ratiometric detection method was developed towards DPA. The fluorescence intensity ratio of F616nm/F461nm gradually increased with the increased concentration of DPA, and showed a good linearity from 0.1 to 750 nM, with a lowest detection limit of 0.1 nM (Fig. 3A). This is much more sensitive than the 60 μM infectious dose of the spores [1]. The results are comparable to previous reports for the DPA detection (Table S2). The fluorescence intensity ratio versus the concentration of DPA were fitted into a linear equation of F616nm/F461nm = 0.003[DPA] (nM) + 0.396, with a correlation coefficient R of 0.999. The method also showed excellent selectivity towards some interferents (Fig. 3B). The application of the DPA detection approach was investigated in natural water and FBS samples. As shown in Table 1 , no DPA was found in real samples, the recoveries of the spiked samples with DPA varied from 101.1% to 110.9%, and the RSD of each sample was below 3.5%, suggesting the applicability and high reproducibility of the method to real samples.

In order to verify the detection mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime of the FMn-CDs with different concentrations of DPA was tested. As shown in Figs. 3C and 3D, the lifetime of the blue fluorescence of the FMn-CDs remained constant (2.333, 2.337 and 2.396 ns) with the increase of the DPA concentration (0, 10 and 100 nM), indicating that the FMn-CDs acted as a well fluorescence reference signal. At the same time, the red fluorescence of Eu(III)-DPA complex on the FMn-CDs gradually increased (171.9, 237.8 and 279.4 μs) with the increase of DPA concentration (0, 10 and 100 nM). Meanwhile, DPA showed an obvious absorption band at 270 nm (Fig. S9), it can absorb the excitation light and transfer the energy to Eu(III). These results revealed that the detection mechanism was based on the absorption energy transfer emission (AETE) effect from DPA to Eu(III) on the FMn-CDs. As illustrated in Scheme 1, EA, EDTA and Eu(III) were introduced onto the FMn-CDs, and converted into the FMn-CDs-(H2O)3 complex. Due to the nonradiative quenching of the coordinated water molecular to the fluorescence of Eu(III) on the FMn-CDs, the FMn-CDs emit the blue internal fluorescence [8–10]. Upon exposure of DPA, water molecules were excluded by DPA and formed the FMn-CDs-DPA complex, the nonradiative quenching is blocked and the intense red fluorescence at 593 and 616 nm of the Eu(III) increase sharply [2]. With the increase of DPA, the fluorescence of the FMn-CDs gradually varied from blue to red.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection for DPA using the FMn-CDs test papers
ce:section.ce:para: Developing a rapid, convenient and economical method for the DPA detection has received extensive attentions in recent years. Herein, a portable, facile and economical FMn-CDs test paper has been developed for the DPA detection using common fiber filter papers. As shown in Fig. 4 , under a portable 254 nm UV lamp, the FMn-CDs test paper turned an obvious color switching from blue to red immediately upon exposure to a certain concentration of DPA, which can be readily observed by naked eyes. The color changes can be recorded using a smartphone. A color-scanning APP was applied to show the color intensity of the red-green-blue (RGB) intensity of the photos, ensuring the FMn-CDs test paper accessible for on-sit application. The intensity ratio of R/B was calculated to evaluate the fluorescence difference of the FMn-CDs test paper in the different concentrations of DPA. According to the intensity ratio of R/B, a calibration linearity curve of R/B = 0.00631[DPA] + 0.272 could be obtained for the DPA determination range from 1.0 to 200 μM. The detection limit was found to be 1.0 μM. The FMn-CDs test paper showed excellent stability kept in dark at room temperature for months. Coupling with a smartphone, the FMn-CDs test paper reveals great potential in practical applications.

Finally, the FMn-CDs test paper was used in the detection of water and the FBS samples spiked with DPA. As shown in Table 2 , the recoveries of the real sample detection varied from 83.38% to 119.2%, and the RSD was below 11.5%, suggesting its potential application in the near future.

In summary, FMn-CDs were synthesized by post-modification pyrolysis derived Mn-CDs with EA, EDTA and Eu(III). The FMn-CDs emitted intense blue fluorescence and showed potential application in cell imaging. A ratiometric fluorescence detection method towards DPA was developed based on AETE effect using the FMn-CDs. The developed approach showed sensitive responses with a linearity range from 0.1 to 750 nM, with a detection limit of 0.1 nM. In addition, the proposed approach has potential application for visual detection of DPA using the FMn-CDs test paper with a detection limit of 1 μM, suggesting its potential application in real samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by the Guangdong Natural Science Foundation (No. 2017A030310652 and 2017A030313255), Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangzhou City (201804010117), Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangdong Province (No. 2011A060901013) and Guangzhou University’s training program for excellent new-recruited doctors (YB201717).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.03.094.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

J. Electron Spectrosc.
Small molecule-based ratiometric fluorescence probes for cations, anions, and biomolecules
Chem. Soc. Rev.
A ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe based on terbium functionalized carbon dots for highly sensitive detection of an anthrax biomarker
Chem. Commun.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Mingcong Rong received her Ph.D. degree in Xiamen University in 2016. She moved to Guangzhou University in 2016 as a lecture. Her research interests include synthesis of carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials and their applications in bio/chemical sensing.
